steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: The sentence reads: “Barter economies eschew ______ that requires what economist W.S. Jevons deems a ‘double coincidence of wants.'” The relative clause “that requires...” is restrictive — it identifies which system is meant — so it must attach directly to the noun it modifies (“system”) without any separating punctuation.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "currency, in favor of a direct trade system": The comma after “currency” suggests a parenthetical break, but the phrase “in favor of a direct trade system” is an essential part of the object and should not be separated. ✗
  • "currency in favor of a direct trade system": No punctuation. The full noun phrase “currency in favor of a direct trade system” flows cleanly as the object of “eschew,” and “that requires...” then attaches unambiguously to “system.” ✓
  • "currency, in favor of a direct trade system,": Two commas create a parenthetical, implying the phrase could be removed — but without it, the sentence loses its essential meaning. ✗
  • "currency—in favor of a direct trade system": A dash signals an abrupt break or insertion. The phrase is not an aside; it is central to the meaning, so a dash is not appropriate here. ✗
That matches the correct answer — B is correct.
